description: Strong junior evaluated against three schools spanning the selectivity spectrum: MIT (ULTRA_SELECTIVE, ~4%), UC Berkeley (HIGHLY_SELECTIVE, ~11%), and Ohio State (ACCESSIBLE, ~60%). The cross-school assertion verifies that the same student's category-rank is monotonically non-decreasing as acceptance_rate increases — a test of the algorithm's internal consistency that no per-school invariant can catch.
business_rationale: An algorithm that gives the same student a worse fit category at a less-selective school is broken in a way operators would notice and lose trust over (e.g., 'Sam is a SAFETY at MIT but TARGET at Ohio State?'). The per-school invariants in Phase 1 catch floor + ceiling violations independently; this cross-school scenario catches the harder bug class — internal inconsistency across tiers. It's our first algorithmic-quality test (vs. structural).
tests: Each per-school compute_fit step satisfies all 7 invariants (category whitelist, match-% range, floor, ceiling, factor bounds, advisory blocks)
Cross-school category-rank monotonic with selectivity: MIT (most selective) cannot rank higher than UC Berkeley, which cannot rank higher than Ohio State
Same student profile evaluated against 3 distinct selectivity tiers — exercises the algorithm's internal consistency
